<jats:label>0)</jats:label>
                <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>Determining “excess mortality” makes it possible to compare the burden of disasters between countries and over time, and thus also to evaluate the success of mitigation measures. However, the debate on Covid-19 has exposed that calculations of excess mortalities vary considerably depending on the method and its specification. Moreover, it is often unclear what exactly is meant by “excess mortality”. We define excess mortality as the excess over the number of deaths that would have been expected counter-factually, i.e. without the catastrophic event in question. That is, we include all normally occurring flu and heat waves, which are excluded by some authors with the consequence that they almost always record low expected values and correspondingly high excess mortality rates. Based on this definition, we use a very parsimonious calculation method that is easy to understand even for laypersons, namely the linear extrapolation of death figures from previous years to determine the excess mortality during the Covid-19 pandemic. But unlike other literature on this topic, we first evaluated and optimised the specification of our method using a larger historical data set in order to identify and minimise estimation errors and biases. The result shows that the excess mortality rates continuously published by international statistical offices – OECD and Eurostat – are often inflated and would have exhibited considerable excess mortalities in many countries and periods before Covid-19, if this value had already been of public interest at that time. It also reveals that mortality rates already fluctuated strongly in the past and that in a third of the countries studied, individual values from the past exceed the current fluctuations due to the Covid-19 pandemic. Three conclusions can be drawn from this study and its findings: 1) All calculation methods for current figures should first be evaluated against past figures. 2) The definition of excess mortality used should be made explicit. 3) Statistical offices should provide more realistic estimates.</jats:p>
title: Calculation and meaning of “excess mortality”: A comparison of Covid- and pre-Covid mortality data in 31 Eurostat countries from 1965 to 2021

Sometimes the number of excess deaths is related to 100,000 inhabitants (e.g. Wang et al. 2022). For comparisons between countries, it should then be noted that there are large differences in normal mortality; i.e. an excess mortality of 100 per 100,000 in a country with a low normal mortality of e.g. 1,000 per 100,000 would account for an excess mortality of 10 percent, while in a country with a higher normal mortality of 2,000 per 100,000 it would only account for an excess mortality of 5 percent.
In our sample, annual mortality figures across all 31 countries increase from 3.67 million in 1960 to 4.78 million in 2019, or by about 30 percent.
The lowest prominently published estimate for Germany is 6,300 excess deaths for 2020 and 23,400 for 2021 (de Nicola et al. 2022a and 2022b).

France is indicated there partly with and partly without its non-European territories. Based on overlaps, a conversion factor was estimated and thus the missing figures – 2013 to 2021 – were imputed for metropolitan France. The year 1960 is missing for Cyprus.

Here we roughly follow the concept of Schöley 2021. Yet, in our case the expected value is in the denominator and not in the numerator, because in this way excess mortality and lower than expected mortality are displayed symmetrically to the expected value. Cf. reference [9] for more detail.
Therefore, only results from the 5-year trend method will be reported in the following.
Wealth is measured as GDP per capita in 2020 in logarithmic form. The correlation coefficient is -0.82, with p<0.001.

The problem of the dependence of the range of variation on population numbers can be adjusted by studentisation; however, the resulting z-scores are not as easily comprehensible in public communication as percentages.

He divides the expected values by the observed values and not vice versa (as is done here in the text). Especially in the case of high deviations, this leads to the problem that excess mortalities and lower than expected mortalities are not represented symmetrically. Example calculation: Expected value = 100; observed value(1) = 40; observed value(2) = 160 (i.e. minus versus plus 60 deaths). Schöley(1)= (40-100)/40= -1.5 = -150%. Schöley(2)=(160-100)/160= 0,375=+37%. Whereas Gill et al.(1)=40/100-1=-0.6=-60%; Gill et al.(2)=160/100-1=0.6=+60%. However, the distortion of this order of magnitude only occur for very high deviations, which are relatively rare.

In Figure 3, it was observed that the amplitude in the lower than expected mortality range is about the same as in the excess mortality range. The amplitude of excess mortalities is generally explained by flu waves in winter and heat waves in summer. But how to explain the approximately equal amplitude in the lower than expected mortality range? Of course, in an estimation method with a bias near zero, excess mortalities and lower than expected mortalities must balance each other out, but one would expect a smaller amplitude in the lower than expected mortality range – a year with high excess mortality would have to be balanced by several years with slightly lower than expected mortality (cf. Vu Manh 2022, figure 1 ).
